quiz_questions.question_text: What is the first step of the scientific method?

options.text: Observation — gathering data about a phenomenon
Building a computer model
Publishing results in a journal
Forming a conclusion

quiz_questions.explanation: The scientific method begins with observation — noticing something interesting or puzzling. In astronomy, observations come from telescopes, satellites, and instruments collecting data from space.

quiz_questions.question_text: What makes a hypothesis 'scientific'?

options.text: It must be proven true before being stated
It must be testable and falsifiable
It must involve expensive equipment
It must be agreed upon by most scientists

quiz_questions.explanation: A scientific hypothesis must be testable — it must make predictions that can be checked against observations. If no possible observation could prove it wrong (falsifiable), it is not scientific.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why can't astronomers bring most of their subjects into a laboratory?

options.text: Stars, galaxies, and planets are too large and distant to bring to a lab
Lab experiments are forbidden in astronomy
Astronomers don't have laboratories
There is no funding for astronomy labs

quiz_questions.explanation: Unlike chemistry or biology, astronomy deals with objects that are enormous and incredibly distant. Scientists cannot bring a star into a lab, so they must observe from afar and use indirect methods.

quiz_questions.question_text: What role do computer simulations play in astronomy?

options.text: They are not used in modern astronomy
They are only used for video games about space
They replace all telescope observations
They model processes that take millions or billions of years, allowing predictions to be tested

quiz_questions.explanation: Computer simulations model galaxy collisions, star formation, and other processes too slow or large to observe directly. Scientists compare simulation results with real observations to test theories.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'peer review' in science?

options.text: Students grade each other's homework
A scientist reviews their own work for errors
A scientist presents findings on social media
Other experts evaluate a scientist's work before it is published

quiz_questions.explanation: Peer review is a quality-control process where independent experts evaluate research methods, data, and conclusions before publication. It helps catch errors and ensures scientific standards.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of these is a testable hypothesis?

options.text: 'Space is beautiful'
'Aliens definitely exist on Mars'
'Stars with more mass burn their fuel faster and have shorter lifetimes'
'The universe has a purpose'

quiz_questions.explanation: A testable hypothesis makes specific, measurable predictions. The statement about stellar mass and lifetime can be tested by observing stars of different masses and measuring their ages and fuel consumption rates.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer predicts that a specific comet will return in 76 years based on orbital calculations. This prediction is:

options.text: A testable prediction based on a scientific model
Impossible to verify
Just a random guess
A religious belief about the future

quiz_questions.explanation: Halley's Comet has a 76-year orbital period. Edmund Halley predicted its return using Newton's gravitational theory — and it came back as predicted in 1758, 1835, 1910, and 1986. This demonstrates the power of predictive science.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why are repeated observations important in astronomy?

options.text: Astronomers are required to observe everything exactly three times
They confirm results and reduce the chance of errors or coincidences
Repeated observations make telescopes more accurate
There is no reason — one observation is always enough

quiz_questions.explanation: Single observations can be affected by instrument errors, atmospheric conditions, or statistical flukes. Repeated observations by multiple teams with different equipment build confidence that results are real.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the cosmic microwave background (CMB) evidence of?

options.text: Radio signals from alien civilizations
Light from the nearest stars
The remnant radiation from the early universe, supporting the Big Bang theory
Microwaves from cell phone towers

quiz_questions.explanation: The CMB is faint radiation filling all of space, left over from about 380,000 years after the Big Bang when the universe cooled enough for atoms to form. Its discovery in 1965 was powerful evidence for the Big Bang model.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens when a well-established scientific theory is contradicted by new evidence?

options.text: Scientists vote on whether to keep or discard it
The theory must be revised or replaced to account for the new evidence
The theory is kept because it is too old to change
The new evidence is always wrong

quiz_questions.explanation: Science is self-correcting. Even well-established theories are revised when contradicted by reliable new evidence. This happened when Einstein's general relativity superseded Newton's gravity for extreme conditions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'hypothesis' in science?

options.text: A proven fact that cannot change
An opinion shared by most people
A question that has no answer
A testable explanation for an observation or phenomenon

quiz_questions.explanation: A hypothesis is an educated, testable explanation. It must make predictions that can be confirmed or falsified by observation or experiment. In astronomy, a hypothesis might predict what we should see when we point a telescope at something.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'scientific theory'?

options.text: A well-tested explanation supported by extensive evidence from multiple lines of inquiry
A law that can never be changed
A personal opinion held by a scientist
An untested guess about how something works

quiz_questions.explanation: In science, 'theory' does not mean 'guess.' A scientific theory like the Big Bang theory or the theory of gravity is supported by vast amounts of evidence and has survived repeated testing. Theories can evolve as new evidence emerges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'falsifiability'?

options.text: Making false claims in a research paper
The possibility that a hypothesis could be proven wrong by evidence
A type of logical error in mathematics
The likelihood that an experiment will fail

quiz_questions.explanation: Falsifiability is a core requirement of science (proposed by Karl Popper). A hypothesis that cannot possibly be disproven by any observation is not scientific. 'There is an invisible dragon in my garage' is unfalsifiable — and therefore not science.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are 'observational constraints' in astronomy?

options.text: Limitations on what astronomers can study due to distance, timescales, and technology
Safety regulations for using telescopes
Rules about where observatories can be built
The maximum number of observations allowed per night

quiz_questions.explanation: Astronomers face unique constraints: objects are too distant to visit, processes take millions of years, and Earth's atmosphere blocks certain wavelengths. These constraints shape how astronomical research is designed and conducted.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'empirical evidence' mean?

options.text: Evidence based on a scientist's personal feelings
Evidence that can only be gathered in a laboratory
Evidence that is kept secret from other researchers
Evidence based on observation or measurement rather than theory alone

quiz_questions.explanation: Empirical evidence comes from direct observation or measurement. In astronomy, this includes telescope images, spectra, brightness measurements, and gravitational wave detections — anything that can be independently verified.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'control variable'?

options.text: A factor kept constant during an experiment so its effect is not confused with the factor being tested
The result that the experiment produces
A random factor that scientists cannot predict
The variable that the scientist changes on purpose

quiz_questions.explanation: In experiments, control variables are held constant so that any changes in the outcome can be attributed to the independent variable being tested. In observational astronomy, controls are harder to establish, making careful data analysis essential.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'scientific model'?

options.text: A photograph taken through a telescope
A simplified representation of a complex system used to make predictions
A physical toy version of a planet
A final answer that can never be changed

quiz_questions.explanation: Scientific models simplify reality to focus on key features. In astronomy, models include mathematical equations for orbits, computer simulations of galaxy formation, and physical models of stellar interiors.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'replicability' mean in science?

options.text: Cloning organisms for biological study
Making exact copies of telescopes
Publishing the same paper in multiple journals
The ability of other scientists to repeat an experiment or observation and get the same results

quiz_questions.explanation: Replicability is essential for confidence in scientific results. If only one team gets a result and no one else can reproduce it, the finding is considered unreliable. In astronomy, multiple observatories often verify important discoveries.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'prediction' in the scientific method?

options.text: A summary of a scientist's career
A guess about the future that cannot be tested
A specific, testable statement about what should be observed if a hypothesis is correct
A conclusion drawn after an experiment is complete

quiz_questions.explanation: A prediction follows logically from a hypothesis. For example, if the Big Bang theory is correct, we should see a uniform background glow of microwave radiation — which is exactly what Penzias and Wilson discovered in 1965 (the CMB).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'dark matter' as a scientific hypothesis?

options.text: An invisible form of matter proposed to explain gravitational effects that visible matter alone cannot account for
The color of deep space, particularly regarding lensing as determined by standard methodology
Matter that exists only in dark places like caves as determined by standard methodology
A type of black hole, particularly regarding gravitational as typically measured in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: Dark matter does not emit, absorb, or reflect light, but its gravitational effects are observed in galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing. It is estimated to make up about 27% of the universe's total mass-energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: A team discovers that galaxy rotation curves don't match predictions from visible matter alone — outer stars orbit too fast. They hypothesize dark matter. What makes this a good hypothesis?

options.text: It makes testable predictions: gravitational lensing effects, galaxy cluster behavior, and CMB patterns should all show extra mass
It is based on a vote among astronomers
It is impossible to ever test because dark matter is invisible
It explains everything perfectly without any further testing needed

quiz_questions.explanation: A good hypothesis makes specific, testable predictions. The dark matter hypothesis predicted gravitational lensing around galaxy clusters and certain patterns in the CMB — both of which were later observed, strengthening the hypothesis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two groups of astronomers publish contradictory measurements of the universe's expansion rate. One says 67 km/s/Mpc, the other 73 km/s/Mpc. What should happen next?

options.text: Both groups should recheck their methods, and additional independent teams should make measurements to resolve the discrepancy
The group with more scientists must be right
They should average the two numbers and accept that answer
Both measurements must be wrong since they disagree

quiz_questions.explanation: This is the real 'Hubble tension' in cosmology. The scientific response is to identify potential sources of systematic error, develop new measurement methods, and gather more data. Disagreements like this often drive major advances in understanding.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student says: 'Evolution is just a theory, so it's just a guess.' Using astronomy as context, explain why this misunderstands the word 'theory.'

options.text: The student is correct — theories are always just guesses when evaluated systematically
Theories become laws once they are proven, particularly regarding theory when measured under controlled conditions
Theories are only used in astronomy, not other sciences following the established framework
In science, a theory is a well-tested explanation supported by extensive evidence — like the Big Bang theory, which is supported by the CMB, galaxy redshifts, and element abundances

quiz_questions.explanation: The everyday meaning of 'theory' (a guess) differs vastly from the scientific meaning (a rigorously tested framework). The Big Bang theory, the theory of gravity, and the theory of evolution are all supported by enormous bodies of evidence.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer claims to have detected signals from an alien civilization. The scientific community asks for independent verification. Why?

options.text: The scientific community already knows aliens don't exist
Only one astronomer is allowed to make discoveries per year
Extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence, and independent replication rules out equipment errors or misinterpretation
Scientists are jealous of the discovery

quiz_questions.explanation: Independent verification is a cornerstone of science. Signals could be caused by equipment malfunction, terrestrial interference, or data processing errors. In 1967, Jocelyn Bell's 'LGM' (Little Green Men) signal turned out to be the first pulsar — a natural phenomenon.

quiz_questions.question_text: A computer simulation predicts that two colliding galaxies should form a specific shape. When astronomers observe actual colliding galaxies, they see that shape. What does this demonstrate?

options.text: Simulations are always perfect representations of reality
The observation must be wrong because simulations are always more accurate
The simulation caused the galaxies to collide
The simulation's underlying physical model (gravity, gas dynamics) is likely correct for this process

quiz_questions.explanation: When observations match simulation predictions, it supports the physics built into the model. However, scientists remain cautious — a model that works in one case may fail in others, so continued testing is always needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Before the discovery of the CMB in 1965, the Big Bang and Steady State theories were both serious contenders for the origin of the universe. How did the CMB help resolve the debate?

options.text: Both theories predicted the CMB equally well
The CMB had nothing to do with either theory
The CMB proved that the Steady State theory was always correct
The Big Bang theory predicted a residual background radiation, while the Steady State theory did not — the CMB's discovery supported the Big Bang

quiz_questions.explanation: The Big Bang theory specifically predicted that the universe should be filled with cooled radiation from its hot early phase. When Penzias and Wilson accidentally detected this microwave background, it was decisive evidence favoring the Big Bang over the Steady State model.

quiz_questions.question_text: An astronomer publishes a paper claiming that a nearby star will explode as a supernova within 100 years. Other astronomers are skeptical. What is the best way to evaluate this claim?

options.text: Wait 100 years to see if it happens, particularly regarding consistent as observed in standard experiments
Accept it because the astronomer has a PhD based on established principles
Reject it because supernovae are rare, particularly regarding claiming according to conventional analysis
Examine the evidence: check the star's mass, evolutionary stage, and compare the analysis to known supernova precursor criteria

quiz_questions.explanation: Critical evaluation involves checking the evidence against established knowledge: Is the star massive enough (typically >8 solar masses)? Is it in the right evolutionary stage? Are the spectroscopic signatures consistent? Science evaluates claims based on evidence, not authority.

quiz_questions.question_text: A high school student discovers what appears to be a new asteroid in telescope images taken for a school project. What should be the next step?

options.text: Keep it secret until they win a science fair
Name it immediately and announce it on social media
Assume it's a camera error and ignore it
Report the observation with precise coordinates so professional observatories can independently confirm it

quiz_questions.explanation: In astronomy, new discoveries (asteroids, comets, supernovae) are reported to the Minor Planet Center or Central Bureau for Astronomical Telegrams. Professional follow-up confirms the discovery and determines the object's orbit. Citizen scientists and students have made real asteroid discoveries this way.

quiz_questions.question_text: A cosmologist builds a model predicting that the universe will expand forever. Another builds a model predicting eventual contraction. Both use valid physics. How does science resolve this?

options.text: By making observations that distinguish the models — such as measuring the expansion rate and how it changes over time
By choosing the model with the more famous author when considering all relevant factors
Science cannot resolve competing models in the context of this analysis
By flipping a coin, particularly regarding supernovae as determined by standard methodology

quiz_questions.explanation: In the late 1990s, observations of distant supernovae revealed that the universe's expansion is accelerating — powered by mysterious 'dark energy.' This observational evidence supported the ever-expanding model and was so important it won the 2011 Nobel Prize in Physics.

quiz_questions.question_text: A journalist writes: 'Scientists can't agree on whether dark matter exists, so science has failed.' Why is this statement misleading?

options.text: Debate and testing of competing ideas is how science works — it's a sign of active research, not failure
The journalist is correct — disagreement means science is broken
Scientists never disagree about anything
Dark matter has already been conclusively proven

quiz_questions.explanation: Scientific debate is healthy and productive. The dark matter question involves active research with multiple lines of evidence being tested. Disagreement drives deeper investigation and better experiments. Science progresses through questioning, not consensus by authority.
